content:
# Exodus Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34v–35r (PDF pp. 67–68); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 47–48 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_007_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_007_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_007_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "See, I have set you as God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet.

**2** You shall speak all that I command you, and Aaron your brother shall speak to Pharaoh, that he send out the sons of Israel from his land.

**3** And I will make hard [**qashah**] the heart of Pharaoh, and I will multiply My signs and My wonders in the land of Egypt.

**4** And Pharaoh will not listen to you, and I will lay My hand upon Egypt, and I will bring out My hosts, My people the sons of Israel, from the land of Egypt by great judgments.

**5** And the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch out My hand over Egypt, and bring out the sons of Israel from their midst."

**6** And Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded them; so they did.

**7** And Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron was eighty-three years old, when they spoke to Pharaoh.

**8** And the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

**9** "When Pharaoh speaks to you, saying, 'Give for yourselves a wonder,' then you shall say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and cast it before Pharaoh,' and it shall become a serpent."

**10** And Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh, and they did so, as the LORD had commanded. And Aaron cast down his staff before Pharaoh and before his servants, and it became a serpent.

**11** And Pharaoh also called for the wise men and the sorcerers, and they also, the magicians of Egypt, did so with their secret arts.

**12** And each man cast down his staff, and they became serpents. But the staff of Aaron swallowed up their staffs.

**13** And the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [**chazaq**], and he did not listen to them, as the LORD had spoken.

**14** And the LORD said to Moses, "The heart of Pharaoh is heavy [**kaved**]; he refuses to send out the people.

**15** Go to Pharaoh in the morning — behold, he goes out to the water — and you shall station yourself to meet him on the bank of the Nile, and the staff that was turned into a serpent you shall take in your hand.

**16** And you shall say to him, 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you, saying: Send out My people, that they may serve Me in the wilderness. And behold, you have not listened until now.

**17** Thus says the LORD: By this you shall know that I am the LORD. Behold, I am striking with the staff that is in my hand upon the waters that are in the Nile, and they shall be turned to blood.

**18** And the fish that are in the Nile shall die, and the Nile shall stink, and the Egyptians shall be unable to drink water from the Nile.'"

**19** And the LORD said to Moses, "Say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt — over their rivers, over their canals, over their pools, and over every gathering of their waters — and they shall become blood.' And there shall be blood in all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone."

**20** And Moses and Aaron did so, as the LORD had commanded. And he lifted up the staff and struck the waters that were in the Nile, before the eyes of Pharaoh and before the eyes of his servants, and all the waters that were in the Nile were turned to blood.

**21** And the fish that were in the Nile died, and the Nile stank, and the Egyptians were not able to drink water from the Nile. And the blood was in all the land of Egypt.

**22** And the magicians of Egypt did so with their secret arts, and the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened [**chazaq**], and he did not listen to them, as the LORD had spoken.

**23** And Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not set his heart even to this.

**24** And all the Egyptians dug around the Nile for water to drink, for they were not able to drink from the waters of the Nile.

**25** And seven days were fulfilled after the LORD struck the Nile.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~34v (PDF p. 67) begins with the conclusion of Exodus 6 and transitions into chapter 7. The chapter opening (7:1) follows immediately from 6:30, as vv. 6:28-7:7 form a continuous commissioning unit. The three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks continues. A setumah (closed paragraph marker) appears after v. 7, separating the commission from the staff-to-serpent narrative. Another setumah appears after v. 13, marking the transition from the sign before Pharaoh to the first plague. The first plague narrative (vv. 14-25) is written as a continuous block through to the end of the chapter. Folio ~35r (PDF p. 68) carries the text forward into Exodus 8. Masorah parva annotations appear in the margins, with notes on several key terms. The Codex Vaticanus (pp. 47-48) was consulted as the primary Greek witness. The text of Exodus 7 begins in the lower portion of page 47, continuing from chapter 6, and extends across the column break onto page 48. The continuous majuscule script preserves the LXX Vorlage, which shows several notable expansions and differences from the MT, particularly in vv. 1, 9, and 19, as detailed in the notes below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"I have set you as God to Pharaoh" (v. 1):** Hebrew netatikha elohim le-Far'oh. The verb natan ("to give, to set, to make") with the accusative elohim ("God/god") creates a striking declaration: Moses is placed in the divine role relative to Pharaoh. This does not mean Moses becomes divine, but that he functions as God's authorized representative with sovereign power over Pharaoh -- just as God stands over all. Aaron then functions as Moses' navi ("prophet"), the one who speaks on his behalf. The parallel is precise: as a prophet speaks for God, so Aaron speaks for Moses-as-God. The LXX renders dedoka se theon Pharao ("I have given you as god to Pharaoh"), preserving the construction. LOGOS renders "God" (capitalized) following the MT's use of elohim without the article, consistent with v. 1's theological weight.

2. **"I will make hard the heart of Pharaoh" (v. 3) -- qashah:** Hebrew va'ani aqsheh et-lev Par'oh. This is the first occurrence of the root q-sh-h ("to be hard, to make hard") in the hardening sequence. The three roots used for Pharaoh's hardening in Exodus are: (a) **chazaq** ("to be strong, to strengthen"), (b) **kaved** ("to be heavy, to make heavy"), and (c) **qashah** ("to be hard, to make hard"). In chapter 7, qashah appears only here (v. 3), with God as subject -- a prospective summary of what will unfold. The verb is Hiphil, causative: "I will make hard." LOGOS renders "make hard" for qashah, distinct from "strengthen" for chazaq and "make heavy" for kaved, to preserve the semantic differentiation of the three roots. The LXX reads ego de sklēruno tēn kardian Pharao ("but I will harden the heart of Pharaoh"), using sklērunō ("to harden"), which maps closely to qashah.

3. **Hardening verb in v. 13 -- chazaq:** Hebrew vayechezaq lev Par'oh. The Qal of chazaq: "the heart of Pharaoh was strong/strengthened." Here the heart is the grammatical subject and no divine agent is named. This is the stative/intransitive use: Pharaoh's heart "was strengthened" -- it held firm, became resolute. LOGOS renders "was strengthened" to distinguish from the active divine causation of v. 3. The LXX reads kai katischysen hē kardia Pharao ("and the heart of Pharaoh was made strong"), using katischuō, the characteristic LXX equivalent for chazaq.

4. **"Heavy" (kaved) in v. 14:** Hebrew kaved lev Par'oh. The adjective kaved ("heavy, weighty") from the root k-b-d describes Pharaoh's heart as sluggish, unresponsive, dull. This is the same root that produces kavod ("glory, weight") -- Pharaoh's heart is "weighted down" with its own self-importance and stubbornness. LOGOS renders "is heavy" to preserve the root distinction. This is a divine description of Pharaoh's existing state, not an act of divine causation. The LXX reads bebarētai hē kardia Pharao ("the heart of Pharaoh has been made heavy"), using bareō ("to weigh down"), which corresponds well to kaved.

5. **Hardening verb in v. 22 -- chazaq:** Hebrew vayechezaq lev Par'oh. Identical construction to v. 13. Again the heart "was strengthened" after the magicians replicate the sign -- Pharaoh's heart grows firm upon seeing his own magicians match the plague. LOGOS renders consistently with v. 13.

6. **"Serpent" -- tannin (v. 9, 10, 12) vs. nachash (4:3):** Hebrew tannin. In Moses' earlier encounter at the burning bush (4:3), his staff became a nachash ("snake, serpent"). Here before Pharaoh, the word is tannin, a distinct term denoting a great serpent, sea creature, or dragon-like being. The tannin carries mythological weight: it is the chaos creature of the deep (cf. Genesis 1:21, Isaiah 27:1, Ezekiel 29:3 where Pharaoh himself is called "the great tannin who lies in the midst of his rivers"). The choice of tannin before Pharaoh is thus deliberate -- Aaron's staff becomes the very creature associated with Pharaoh's own mythological power, and it swallows the Egyptian replicas. LOGOS renders "serpent" for both tannin and nachash in the main text (as English lacks a single-word equivalent that captures tannin's full range), but notes the distinction here. The LXX renders drakon ("dragon, serpent") for tannin, preserving its mythological resonance.

7. **"With their secret arts" (vv. 11, 22):** Hebrew belahateyhem. The noun lahat (or lahit) refers to "secret arts, enchantments" -- possibly related to lahat ("flame, flashing") suggesting the concealment or dazzling associated with magical practice. Some scholars connect it to the "flaming sword" (lahat hacherev) of Genesis 3:24. The Egyptian magicians (chartummim, from Egyptian hry-tp, "chief lector priest") employ these arts to replicate the signs. LOGOS renders "secret arts" consistently, following the KJV/NASB tradition. The LXX renders tais pharmakeiais autōn ("with their sorceries"), using pharmakeia, the root of English "pharmacy" -- originally denoting drug-based magical practice.

8. **"The staff that was turned into a serpent" (v. 15):** Hebrew hammateh asher nehpakh lenachash. Note that here the text reverts to nachash, not tannin. The staff in Moses' hand (as opposed to Aaron's) is identified by its original transformation at the burning bush (4:3), where nachash was used. This subtle distinction between Moses' staff (nachash) and Aaron's staff (tannin) reinforces the different contexts: private theophany vs. public confrontation with Pharaoh. LOGOS translates "serpent" in both cases but records the distinction.

9. **"Their rivers, their canals, their pools, and every gathering of their waters" (v. 19):** Hebrew al-neharotam, al-ye'oreyhem, al-agmeyhem, ve'al kol-miqveh meymeyhem. This fourfold enumeration covers all categories of Egyptian water: neharot ("rivers," the branches of the Nile Delta), ye'orim ("canals," from Egyptian itrw, the word for the Nile itself, here in the plural for irrigation channels), agamim ("pools, marshes, standing water"), and miqveh mayim ("gathering of waters," any collected body of water). The comprehensiveness emphasizes that no water source in Egypt is exempt. LOGOS renders each term distinctly. The LXX expands slightly: kai epi potamous autōn kai epi tas diōrugas autōn kai epi ta helē autōn kai epi pan sunestēkos hudōr autōn ("and upon their rivers and upon their canals and upon their marshes and upon every standing water of theirs").

10. **"In vessels of wood and in vessels of stone" (v. 19):** Hebrew uvа'etsim uva'avanim. Literally "and in the woods and in the stones," but the context demands "in [vessels of] wood and in [vessels of] stone" -- even water stored in wooden and stone containers turns to blood. This extends the plague beyond natural water sources to stored water, making it inescapable. The LXX renders en te tois xulois kai en tois lithois ("both in the wooden [vessels] and in the stones"), making the implied "vessels" explicit.

11. **"He did not set his heart even to this" (v. 23):** Hebrew velo-shat libbo gam-lazot. The idiom shit lev ("to set the heart [upon]") means to pay attention, to take to heart. Pharaoh literally "did not place his heart even to this" -- he refused to give the plague any serious consideration. The word gam ("even, also") intensifies the dismissal: not even this moved him. LOGOS preserves the Hebrew idiom. The LXX renders ouk epestēsen ton noun autou oude epi toutō ("he did not set his mind even upon this"), using nous ("mind") for lev ("heart").

12. **"And all the Egyptians dug around the Nile" (v. 24):** Hebrew vayachperu khol-Mitsrayim sevivot haYe'or. The verb chaphar ("to dig") indicates desperate excavation along the riverbanks, seeking subsurface water that might be filtered through the soil and thus drinkable. This detail reveals both the severity of the plague and human resourcefulness under divine judgment. The Egyptians do not cry out to their gods or to Pharaoh -- they simply dig. LOGOS preserves the vivid image.

13. **"Seven days were fulfilled" (v. 25):** Hebrew vayimmale shiv'at yamim. The verb male' ("to be full, to be fulfilled") with "seven days" gives the duration of the first plague. The number seven carries completeness -- the plague runs its full, divinely appointed course. This verse also functions as a literary bridge to the second plague (frogs, 8:1[7:26 in MT]). LOGOS renders "were fulfilled" rather than "passed" to preserve the verb's semantic range.

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 7

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | Subject/Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | **qashah** | Hiphil (causative) | God hardens Pharaoh's heart | "I will make hard the heart of Pharaoh" |
| 7:13 | **chazaq** | Qal (stative) | Pharaoh's heart was strengthened | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" |
| 7:14 | **kaved** | Adjective (stative) | Pharaoh's heart is heavy | "the heart of Pharaoh is heavy" |
| 7:22 | **chazaq** | Qal (stative) | Pharaoh's heart was strengthened | "the heart of Pharaoh was strengthened" |

*Note: In chapter 7, all three hardening roots appear. Qashah (v. 3) occurs only once in the entire Exodus hardening cycle and is spoken by God as a prospective declaration. Chazaq appears twice (vv. 13, 22), both times in the stative Qal with Pharaoh's heart as subject -- no divine agent is named. Kaved appears once (v. 14), as a divine diagnosis of Pharaoh's existing condition. The pattern in chapter 7 is notable: God announces He will cause hardening (qashah, v. 3), but the narrative descriptions present Pharaoh's heart as hardening on its own (chazaq, vv. 13, 22) or as already in a heavy state (kaved, v. 14). The explicit divine hardening (Piel of chazaq or kaved with God as subject) does not begin until later in the plague cycle.*

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Consistent throughout |
| YHWH | the LORD | Small-caps convention; represents the Tetragrammaton |
| Elohim | God | v. 1 "I have set you as God to Pharaoh" |
| qashah (heart) | make hard | v. 3; Hiphil causative, God as agent |
| chazaq (heart) | strengthen / was strengthened | vv. 13, 22; Qal stative, heart as subject |
| kaved (heart) | heavy / make heavy | v. 14; adjective, describing Pharaoh's state |
| tannin | serpent | vv. 9, 10, 12; distinct from nachash (4:3); see note |
| nachash | serpent | v. 15; the burning-bush transformation |
| chartummim | magicians | vv. 11, 22; Egyptian hry-tp, "chief lector priest" |
| lahatim | secret arts | vv. 11, 22; enchantments, magical concealment |
| ye'or | Nile | Throughout; from Egyptian itrw |
| shefatim gedolim | great judgments | v. 4; judicial acts against Egypt |
| mofet | wonder | v. 9; sign-act demanding a response |
| matteh | staff | Throughout; Moses' and Aaron's rod |
| dam | blood | First plague; vv. 17-21 |

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 7 generally follows the MT closely in narrative structure but shows several characteristic differences. In v. 1, the LXX adds kai Aaron ho adelphos sou estai sou prophētēs ("and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet"), matching the MT. In v. 9, the LXX reads somewhat differently in the phrasing of Pharaoh's demand. The plague narrative (vv. 14-25) is rendered faithfully, though the LXX consistently uses sklērunō for qashah (v. 3) and katischuō/krataioō for chazaq (vv. 13, 22), preserving the root distinction in Greek as LOGOS does in English. The term tannin is rendered drakon throughout (vv. 9, 10, 12), preserving its mythological dimension. One notable difference: in v. 18, the LXX adds that the Egyptians "will not be able" (ou dunēsontai) to drink, using a stronger modal verb than the MT's simple imperfect. The seven-day notice in v. 25 appears in both traditions, though the LXX places it slightly differently in relation to the following plague announcement.

--- LOGOS
